FT_IC1.evidence: Sample of 10,588 participants from ABCD Study at 2-year follow-up assessment

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone ownership and age of first smartphone acquisition (parent-reported); qualifying per B1 rule as separate smartphone variable

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects logistic regression models testing associations between smartphone ownership/acquisition and health outcomes (depression, obesity, insufficient sleep)

FT_IC4.evidence: Odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals reported in main text: smartphone ownership vs depression OR 1.31 (95% CI 1.05-1.63), obesity OR 1.40 (95% CI 1.20-1.63), insufficient sleep OR 1.62 (95% CI 1.46-1.79); age of acquisition ORs reported; supplemental tables provide additional coefficients

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis; mixed-effects models testing prospective associations across 3 analyses: (1) smartphone ownership at age 12 with outcomes at age 12; (2) age of acquisition with outcomes at age 12; (3) recent smartphone acquisition among non-owners at age 12 with outcomes at age 13

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of preprint, abstract, or duplicate submission

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone is the INDEPENDENT variable in all analyses predicting health outcomes (depression, obesity, insufficient sleep). There are NO analyses testing smartphone/social media as dependent variable. Does not satisfy condition (a) of FT_EC7.
notes: Paper 431 is a clear inclusion: smartphone as independent variable with health outcomes as dependent variable. Multiple sensitivity analyses and E-value analyses reported.

FT_IC1.evidence: Sample of 11,875 participants from ABCD Study; longitudinal data from baseline (age 10), 1-year follow-up (age 11), and 2-year follow-up (age 12)

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media explicitly included as distinct lifestyle factor in analyses (Figure 2, Figure 3, and text: 'duration of screen use (distinguishing between social media, social engagement, passive watching, and active gaming)'); social media is qualifying per definition of platform-specific/general social media use

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed models and mediation analyses examining longitudinal associations between neurodevelopmental spectrum and psychiatric dimensions; single-mediation and adjusted mediation models with indirect effects estimated via bootstrap; mediation pathways include environmental and lifestyle factors (including social media) as mediators of associations between neurodevelopmental traits and mental health outcomes

FT_IC4.evidence: Standardized beta coefficients (β) reported with standard errors and p-values in Table 2; effect sizes shown in Figures 2 and 3; example from text: 'neurodevelopmental factor explained 41% of the variance'; Table 2 shows direct effects and mediation models with β values for each outcome at ages 11 and 12

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis; longitudinal mediation models examining associations between neurodevelopmental spectrum (extracted via confirmatory factor analysis at age 10) and psychiatric dimensions at ages 11 and 12, with environmental and lifestyle factors (including social media) tested as potential mediators

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of duplicate submission; appears in JAACAP Open as final published article

FT_EC7_reason: Paper tests neurodevelopmental traits as independent variables predicting psychiatric outcomes, with environmental/lifestyle factors (social media, sleep, family conflict, school involvement) as mediators. Social media is NOT the sole dependent variable, and analyses include non-demographic predictors (neurodevelopmental spectrum). Does not satisfy condition (a) of FT_EC7 (SM as dependent variable in every analysis). Additionally, per B18 logic: paper has both SM-as-IV (mediation analysis) and non-SM outcomes, disqualifying FT_EC7.
notes: Paper 122 is a clear inclusion: social media is tested as mediator of associations between neurodevelopmental traits and mental health outcomes in original ABCD analysis. All six full-text inclusion criteria met.
